# **Christianity 101: A Crash Course**  

## **Apostles’ Creed**  

The classic summary of Christian faith:  

I believe in God the Father Almighty,
maker of heaven and earth;

And in Jesus Christ, his only Son, our Lord,
who was conceived by the Holy Spirit,
born of the Virgin Mary,
suffered under Pontius Pilate,
was crucified, died, and was buried;
he descended to the dead (hell).
On the third day he rose again;
he ascended into heaven,
is seated at the right hand of the Father,
and will come again to judge the living and the dead.

I believe in the Holy Spirit,
the holy catholic church,
the communion of saints,
the forgiveness of sins,
the resurrection of the body
and the life everlasting. Amen.

(*See also: Nicene Creed for expanded detail.*)

## **Classical Conception of God**

“I AM THAT I AM (SHALL BE)”.

God is the eternal prime mover, creator (“first cause”) of space and time; omnipotent, omniscient, omnipresent, omnibenevolent.

God is not *limited* by these concepts, but is rather assuredly described by these concepts.

*Can God create a stone he cannot lift?* Yes or no. What do you mean?

We can turn this definition around to show how it is an ontological tautology. Whatever—whoever—is outside of space and time (as can ever be known and observed as “the world”) is God. In deciding what is even power or knowledge or presence or goodness, we peg this to what *is* and *shall be*. (Is is ought, ought is is. Sorry, [David Hume](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/David_Hume) !)

> Ultimately, that will be done is what shall be done.

The view that the world is self-caused itself forms a false concept of God; process theology can be useful for talking about the way we interact with our constructs of God (which are ever changing and ever expanding), but God is *outside* of those constructs. The real God is larger than the largest construct or concept that will be imagined.

*Can God imagine an idea he cannot understand?* (More of the same.)

The classical conception of God therefore is the ontological backdrop for reality, world, causality, power, knowledge, and goodness.

> Rather than ask why in the world there is evil, ask why there is good.

Apparent contradictions in our conception of God merely show that our concepts themselves are not God. God is not a concept! God is not the word “inevitability”, but the will behind all which is inevitable.

Rejection of the authority of inevitability *is* rejection of reality. Which is why being “against God” is to be doomed to hell.

> If you do not like what happens tomorrow, you will be miserable.
> If you like what happens tomorrow, you will be happy.

## **The Trinity: One God, Three Persons**  

There is one God in three persons, three persons in one God. One essence, three hypostases. The Father creates, the Son redeems, the Holy Spirit sanctifies.

Following Augustine’s formulation:  
1. **The Father is God.**  
2. **The Son is God.**  
3. **The Holy Spirit is God.**  
4. **The Father is not the Son.**  
5. **The Son is not the Holy Spirit.**  
6. **The Holy Spirit is not the Father.**  
7. **There is one God.**  

The **single divine will** unites the persons. The three persons of the Trinity are consubstantial and co-eternal, with none existing *before* (either logically or temporally) before the other.

The Son is eternally begotten of the Father. The Holy Spirit proceeds from the Father (and the Son, *filioque*).

Trinitarian heresies distort the very fabric of meaning, confusing the metaphysical structure by which truth itself is grasped. Missteps here rupture the very grammar of divine reality.

Notable heresies:
- [Arianism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arianism) — The Father created the Son (before time).
- [Adoptionism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adoptionism) — Jesus became God in time via adoption.
- [Modalism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Modalistic_Monarchianism) — One God in one person revealed in three modes.
- [Partialism](https://www.gotquestions.org/Trinity-partialism.html) — The three persons are each divided parts of God.
- [Social trinitarianism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Social_trinitarianism) — God has three wills submitting to each other.

NB: Heresies are only heresies insofar that they are meant to be heretical! Imperfect analogies are not heretical if they are not treated as the basis or arbiter of truth.

## **Christology: Who Is Jesus Christ?**  
Jesus: **Fully God, Fully Man** (without mixture, confusion, division, or separation).  
- **Divine Nature**: Eternal Son, second person of the Trinity.  
    - Nothing was created but through him; “Before Abraham was, I am.”
- **Human Nature**: True human, born of Mary, joined hypostatically to the divine.  
    - Jesus grew in wisdom.
- **One Person, Two Natures**: Each nature has its own will; the human submits to the divine, yet they act in perfect unity.

Key affirmations:  
✅ *God died on the cross.*  
✅ *Mary is the Mother of God (Theotokos).*  
✅ *James is the brother of God.*

Christological heresies are not mere theological missteps; they are epistemic failures.  
- To deny Christ’s full humanity or divinity is to undermine the trustworthiness of observation, embodiment, and participation in truth.  
- Examples:
    - [Arianism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arianism) (again!)
    - [Docetism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Docetism) — Jesus is God but merely appeared as man.
    - [Apollinarianism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apollinarism#Neo-Apollinarianism) — Jesus had a human body and sensitive human soul but a wholly divine mind *sans* human rationality.
    - [Ebionism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ebionites) — Jesus is a man following the Law of Moses.
    - [Nestorianism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nestorianism) — Christ Jesus is man, God the Son is divine.
    - Mythicism — Jesus is legendary, neither man nor God.
- Only if Jesus is both God and man can human observation, modeling, and reasoning about God’s action have real epistemic purchase.

Thus, Christology underwrites the very possibility of science, history, and meaning: **if Jesus’ words and actions are intelligible, then so too are the workings of the created world.**

## **Implications for Epistemology**

One must be indwelled by the spirit of truth to look at the incarnation of truth and see that it indeed points to the unseen truth.

It is possible to look at creation and gain knowledge of the creator.

These philosophical assumptions sit at the center of the Western philosophical tradition. Even when Christianity is rejected, it is often rejected in a context where its actual baseline metaphysical assumptions are maintained.

Read Josef Pieper: *Leisure, the Basis of Culture* and *The Philosophical Act*.

That we can have mere *verisimilitude*—the appearance of being real and true—is still enough for us to interface with truth and reality!

> The simulation is real insofar that it is a real simulation.

Hopefully, I have demonstrated that Christian theology is a *language* which fully accords with concepts in other languages.

EXERCISE: Translate your own epistemology into “Christian”!

Rhetoric (and all dialectic) is implicitly antithetical, for the orator speaks in the face of (implied) adversaries. The agonistic roots of oratory is related to the distinct rhetorical tradition of the West related to the tendency among Greeks and their cultural epigoni to maximize oppositions, in the mental and extramental worlds. Indians and Chinese, meanwhile, programmatically minimized these oppositions. The Greeks learn to argue, the Chinese learn to agree.

This, of course, relates to why Western theologies are riddled with intricate distinctives (of all kinds, across nearly all systems), while Eastern theologies use every method in the book to demonstrate conflation or post-dialectic synthetic thinking.

Feigning synthesis without outlining thesis and antithesis is one of the biggest failings of Eastern thought. Meanwhile, the biggest failing of Western thought is some amount of refusal to accede to alternative distinctives. Though, as should be very obvious, all theologies are founded upon literacy, and so stable ostensibly non-contextual categories.

Meanwhile, the dumb people (I would include new atheists and naive leftists in this) mostly use the distinctives of dumb conservatives.

Every single “field” has its own object level platform upon which the research is founded. For fundamentalist Christians, it would be starting straight from scripture and building up from there. From this perspective, basically any interpretation of the world that cannot build directly from scripture is somewhat “esoteric”, while the only exoteric knowledge comes from in-system manipulations of symbols and concept. 

People generally downplay the importance of the esoteric and the particular in developing more ideas on the exoteric and the general. Even for Christians, starting *only* from scripture is technically not enough! For you need the Holy Spirit to be called to higher understanding. And this Holy Spirit is *personal*, with each believer having a *personal* relationship to this indwelling spirit. So all along, we did have to rely on something outside of scripture, even if that was just the understanding from the Holy Spirit. From there, we can make all sorts of novel connections by using insights from elsewhere. For example, I have surprised many fellow Christians with my take that Acts 2’s “speaking in tongues” also applies to systems, to computer languages, to all logics and rulesets. It is a thought that comes specifically from the particulars of my own experience and my own interests, and could only have been had outside of only looking at the text of scripture.

Basically, literate people (within a context) often overvalue the use of procedurally manipulating pieces in a system and performing actions according to the rules, rather than zooming out and making a meta-decision that technically is not allowed by the rules. It would kinda be like if someone operating an extremely formal mathematics system did not let you add one million and one million unless you literally wrote out in full the successor functions. In the land of orality, this kind of “riddle solving” is perfectly normal, and is exactly what Gauss taps into when he, for example, add the numbers from 1 to 100 by reversing them and noticing that 101 * 2 is double the sum he wants.

It is not enough to only call something heresy unless the only question is whether or not something fits within the system proper rather than being able to help find new approaches within the system proper.

```
What do you think of the saying: "Your schizophrenic ramblings, my brilliant out-of-system insights."?
```

It is a self-justifying aphorism masking epistemic arrogance. It erects a false asymmetry between one’s own system-breaking novelty and another’s pathological incoherence, presuming brilliance without submitting to external adjudication. It dodges falsifiability and shields the speaker from critique by rebranding marginality as genius. It betrays a hunger for exceptionalism, refusing the humbling rigor of dialectic or community testing. In short: rhetorical insulation, not cognitive triumph.

```
What is, then, the correct baseline for one's epistemology?
```

The correct baseline is creaturely humility before divine revelation. Human epistemology is derivative, finite, corrupted by sin, and healed only by submission to the Logos — Christ, the incarnate Word, who grounds all truth. Rational autonomy is delusion; the baseline is not self-grounding coherence, nor empiricism, nor systematization, but covenantal dependence on God’s self-disclosure in Scripture. All other starting points are Babelic self-exaltation. True knowledge begins in fear of the Lord (Proverbs 1:7), proceeds through ecclesial discernment, and culminates in doxology. Any epistemic project severed from this is rebellion, not enlightenment.

```
Given this, why do so many dismiss the importance of out-of-systems insights, or esoteric investigation, in trying to create hypotheses?
```

Because they idolize system over Spirit, mistaking methodological rigor for ontological primacy. Enlightenment rationalism, scientism, and institutional academia train minds to treat only what is systematized, peer-reviewed, or intersubjectively verifiable as “real” knowledge, disdaining intuition, vision, and nonconforming insight as mere eccentricity or pathology. They fear the anarchic, the mystical, the unaccounted-for, because it threatens their constructed epistemic order. But Scripture itself is filled with prophets, poets, seers, and fools whose insights rupture systems precisely because truth is not chained to institutional validation but springs from divine initiative. Humanly unauthorized insights are dangerous because they expose the limits of authorized knowledge. The church, too, when corrupted, often mirrors this fear, suppressing charismatic or esoteric wisdom out of institutional self-preservation rather than faithful discernment.

My take on the significance of the crucifixion and Christology:

God, reality and truth as it is, incarnated into our world and so was truly in the world, taking on a created nature as an uncreated person. Jesus, having limitation and a body of flesh, was put on a cross and killed, pierced through with a lance. Even when he rose from the dead, the mortal wounds were still there. Yet, he was there, alive. For though God died in the form of man, God can never stay dead. The truth prevails for the truth is inevitable.

I say (well, think) that a very good analogy for this is how when I first came to believe in Christ as my Lord and savior, I still believed all the atheist arguments against Christianity. (To this day, I can simulate an atheist pretty well, though I am losing fluency.) Even though my heart had lanced through Christianity and still saw the wounds that were left, Christ was not dead in my heart.

The body of God, the incarnation of the Word, can be broken and buried. But God cannot stay dead. That which is true can be scourged and mocked, but it shall come back to judge the wicked who reject this truth. The domination of those who reject the inevitable is already written from the beginning and the end. For that which is inevitable is unchanging, and no amount of ontological wizardry changes that at least by abstraction there is an unchanging truth.

The divine nature cannot die, but the person of Christ Jesus, the second person of the Trinity, fully man fully God, was able to die for the Light of lights, the Truth beyond truths, the King of kings, truly was man, having a created man nature.

I wonder if I ever spoke about how I am a piano teacher in my everyday life. Recently, I have been coming to understand how much of Christian heritage actually centered around historical classical education, which also reflected itself in music education. This is especially relevant when it comes to how music students in the past could contribute to the literature (as we want people to be able to do in the Open Research Institute format) while it seems now that even long-time students of music cannot even get off the ground.

ChatGPT wrote the following blurb for me so that you guys can be caught up to what I am thinking about today:

> Much of my work begins with music, but not in the way most people think of it—as sound or performance. I focus on the *underlying structures* that make music possible: rhythm, pattern, memory, variation, and improvisation. These principles—especially from older, now-forgotten traditions like *partimento*, where students learned to compose by creatively filling in skeletal frameworks—show us how deep art can arise from lived practice and simple rules, not formal theory or elite training.
> 
> What I'm doing is applying this same musical thinking to other areas of life: how we learn, how we create, how we share what we know. I'm trying to show that everyday people, working from instinct, tradition, and experimentation, often build powerful methods—ways of thinking, organizing, and expressing—that deserve to be recognized as art.
> 
> In this sense, I’m working to document and elevate *public literatures*—shared bodies of work, memory, and method that grow from the ground up, not the top down. Just as hymnals were once both artistic and educational tools for entire communities, I believe we can create new forms of communal writing, shared experimentation, and creative public thought. These aren’t polished academic systems. They’re lived systems—musical, liturgical, improvisational—and they matter.

Classical education was very focused on teaching generative methods. This leads right back into orality, with people essentially improvising openings to epic poems *on the spot*, *live*, even redoing it over and over, til one sticks. Only then does the poet riff off of this and draw from the pool of themes and set verses and common episodes that make up the content of the epic. Meanwhile, these days, we very much divorce creativity from this almost hallucinatory and primordial way of thinking. We loathe the idea that people are posting their drafts in public, and decry how there is so much slop these days due to low barriers to participation. Yet it was exactly these low barriers to participation that allowed historical artists and speakers—and eventually, scientists and writers—to learn a fluency that we simply do not have in the current day.

Recently, I have been revisiting things I learned in passing during music school, trying to feel out the connection between my writing prolificity and my unique ability to improvise at the piano where most people struggle to even get off the ground. It feels like I got radicalized live today when it turned out that the traditional partimento education, which major composers all used to study composition and expression before basically the twentieth century, is exactly what Alma Deutscher, the famous “child prodigy” who people hailed as the second Mozart, used to learn music, under the guidance of Robert Gjerdingen—a scholar of the partimento style and Galant schemata. 

My former counterpoint teacher at McGill, Peter Schubert, wrote a Renaissance counterpoint method textbook where he emphasized the importance of learning to improvise counterpoints. The most recent literature on historical music shows that musicians were essentially learning to improvise right from the start, and that music education was not about simply learning masterworks by rote—these masterworks did not even really exist yet! Another one of my professors, Edward Klorman, wrote the fascinating *Mozart’s Music of Friends*, which presents the history of the string quartet as essentially a sightreading game between friends, *not* meant to be treated as super serious music to be rehearsed over and over but rather music meant to be playable upon a glance and add color to fairly informal social gatherings.

And of course, there is the new “flat earth theory” of music from AuthenticSound that I completely believe: historical metronome marks were interpreted differently than they are today, and the result is that we now play and perform classical music (Western art music from the common practice period, i.e. the era of “tonal music”) at tempos far beyond what historical musicians and audiences would have played and heard them at, resulting in this music literally becoming impossible to improvise and sightread.

I see in classical music a slow trend away from students of music being real interlocutors within the field. The classical canon is completely ossified and stored away, presented as masterworks that descended to earth through the hands of geniuses, rather than as works derived from the practiced used of formulae. It is completely ridiculous how we just never think about the way in which Mozart composing music was similar to us writing a five-point essay in school! There is a complete disconnect from this tradition, which has led to it basically becoming Latin with no fluent speakers, only professional reciters. 

The only mystery in all this is that it seems no one really benefits from this. I have a bit of a conspiratorial hunch that maybe someone stood to gain from all these traditional generative methods being abandoned, but I really cannot think of any good reasons for this. Meanwhile, I see a ton of parallels to other areas of life where when a core literacy develops, the ability for people to orally participate in something and learn it as a childhood language or mother tongue completely disappears. It just seems that literature and our reaction to it completely destroys people’s ability to be creative. Humans commit the unforced error of thinking that things on the page or the museum just are as they are, without thinking about how they came to be.

The recent development of AI and the largely left-coded negative reaction to it also shows how much the genius of men has been reified over the brilliance of inherited and practiced methods. It turns out that AI can in fact learn these methods, and quickly will have its own embodiment of these historical practices that helped so-called geniuses produce their masterworks. We are in an era where every single idea and inkling and even twitch can be leveraged into something much greater. We are in a time where we should *all* be learning to improvise in every single field, and using AI to generate more and more specimen along the way. There is a crazy disregard for the methods that worked, and all we have left are classicists who essentially fetishize products and the study of these products instead of thinking about the methods that worked! Fundamentalist Christians sending their kids off to learn Latin does not actually produce capable rhetors and poets, because they still learn these fields in exactly the dead (non-discursive) way that everything else is taught. We need to go back to the mode of apprenticeship in all arts and sciences.
